content.full_text:
April 5, 1976

Mr. Lingel H. Winters  
Counsel for the Fred Furth  
for State Senate Committee  
2900 Shasta Road  
Berkeley, CA 94706  

Dear Mr. Winters:

This letter confirms our telephone conversation of Friday, April 2, 1976, concerning the spaghetti dinner to be held by the Fred Furth for State Senate Committee. The price of the dinner will be five dollars ($5.00) per person and the committee would like to collect cash to cover the dinner price. You asked whether the committee must keep records of every person who purchases a ticket in order to ascertain whether or not contributions totaling $50 or more have been received from that person. Reports filed under the Political Reform Act must be compiled with "reasonable diligence". Gov. Code Section 81004. However, the standard of "reasonable diligence" should not be interpreted to deter a committee from selling buttons and bumper stickers, "passing the hat" or carrying another activities that raise cash in small amounts. Consequently, the standard of "reasonable diligence" does not require sponsors of the dinner to keep track of every person who purchases a ticket in order to comply with the reporting requirements of the Act. Clearly, if a person purchases ten tickets the committee must keep a record of the donor's name, address and other information required by Gov. Code Section 84210(g) because that person has made a contribution of $50. In addition, you may want to establish an informal guideline for the committee and recommend that it should keep records of anyone who buys, for example, five or more tickets. In this way the committee can would be able to make an inquiry to determine whether a person had contributed $50 or more to the campaign when there is some probability that the contributor might have donated $50, but to avoid the necessity of keeping records of every person who attends the dinner.

I hope this information will be of assistance.

Sincerely,

Natalie E. West  
Staff Counsel
